text: Hi all, this seventy-year-old gentleman presented to our emergency department with upper abdominal pain of one week duration. On examination of the patient, tachycardia and tachypnea was there and there was severe tenderness in right hypochondrium and his total count was fourteen thousand. A CECT was taken and showed distended gallbladder with two large pericholecystic collections. Our initial plan was a nematogated drainage of the collection and a cholecystostomy. And we discussed the same with our interventional radiologist. Since the collection is multiloculated and multiple in number, the approach was a bit difficult. So we decided to a laparoscopic drainage of the collections and a cholecystostomy if possible.

The initial laparoscopic view shows the left lobe of the liver and gallbladder covered with omentum, which is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon introduces a suction apparatus through a port in the right hypochondrium, below the right costal margin, to separate the omental adhesions. Pus is visible as it is suctioned out, with some collected in a syringe for culture and sensitivity testing.

The surgeon visualizes the fundus of the gallbladder and attempts to separate the omentum from the gallbladder using blunt dissection through a newly created 5mm port in the epigastrium, without employing any energy sources.
The surgeon performs a thorough peritoneal lavage using eight to ten liters of normal saline, visibly irrigating the abscess cavity within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a cholecystostomy by introducing an SPC trocar to pierce the gallbladder. After removing the trocar, a 14 French Foley's tube is inserted into the gallbladder for drainage.
The surgeon performs another wash of the surgical area and inserts a 32 French drainage tube, positioning it in the sub-hepatic space.
